grammar.title: Imperative ‘obedezcan’
grammar.detail: ustedes-imperative of obedecer. The -zc- stem (obedezco, obedezca, obedezcan) is shared by many -cer verbs after a vowel.

grammar.title: ‘en el Señor’
grammar.detail: ‘in the Lord’ — qualifies the kind of obedience expected: obedience offered to parents inside the larger frame of obedience to Christ.

expression.phrase: obedezcan en el Señor a sus padres
expression.note: Word order — direct object ‘a sus padres’ comes after the prepositional ‘en el Señor’. Spanish gives you a lot of flexibility here; NVI front-loads the spiritual frame.

grammar.title: Tú-imperative ‘Honra’
grammar.detail: Affirmative tú-imperative of honrar is identical to the 3rd-sg present indicative form: honra. The verse quotes the Decalogue, addressed to each individual.

grammar.title: Dashes for parenthesis
grammar.detail: Spanish uses raya (—) like English em-dashes to set off an aside: ‘— que es el primer mandamiento con promesa —’.

expression.note: Paul’s comment on Exodus 20:12: the fifth commandment is unique in pairing a directive with a specific blessing.

grammar.title: ‘para que’ + subjunctive
grammar.detail: ‘para que te vaya bien y disfrutes…’ — ‘para que’ (so that / in order that) always triggers the subjunctive. ‘vaya’ and ‘disfrutes’ are both present subjunctive.

grammar.title: Indirect object ‘te vaya bien’
grammar.detail: Literally ‘so that it goes well to you’. ‘te’ is the indirect object — the experiencer of the well-going.

expression.note: Quotes Deuteronomy 5:16. Paul (and modern Spanish translators) keep the OT promise wording intact — honour leads to flourishing.
